coredata.dc:title: Construction of carbon nitride-based heterojunction as photocatalyst for peroxymonosulfate activation: Important role of carbon dots in enhancing photocatalytic activity

coredata.dc:description: Strategic combination of carbon dots (CDs) and carbon nitride (CN) has provided an appealing strategy to synthesize metal-free carbonaceous catalyst for environmental-concerned photocatalytic applications. However, there still exists an ambiguous cognition on the relationship between functional structures of CDs and enhanced photocatalytic activity of CDs/CN composites. Herein, different types of functionalized CDs were physically integrated with CN matrix to construct CDs/CN heterojunctions. By systematically investigating the efficiency of CDs/CN-catalyzed peroxymonosulfate (PMS) in p-nitrophenol degradation, we revealed that the CDs enriched with carboxyl groups or doped by pyrrolic-N inclined to attract photogenerated electrons from CN host, and thus contributed to the photocarriers separation. As such, these CDs-imparted active sites can dramatically facilitate PMS activation, and further p-nitrophenol degradation by photogenerated holes and multiple reactive oxygen radicals. Nonetheless, the CDs mainly doped by electron-donating graphitic-N could form electron-rich regions at the interface of CN host upon forming heterojunction, thus making barriers against charge separation in the PMS-mediated oxidation process. Taken together, this study will shed light on the rational design of CDs/CN heterojunctions with high photocatalytic activity.

head.ce:title: Construction of carbon nitride-based heterojunction as photocatalyst for peroxymonosulfate activation: Important role of carbon dots in enhancing photocatalytic activity

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Strategic combination of carbon dots (CDs) and carbon nitride (CN) has provided an appealing strategy to synthesize metal-free carbonaceous catalyst for environmental-concerned photocatalytic applications. However, there still exists an ambiguous cognition on the relationship between functional structures of CDs and enhanced photocatalytic activity of CDs/CN composites. Herein, different types of functionalized CDs were physically integrated with CN matrix to construct CDs/CN heterojunctions. By systematically investigating the efficiency of CDs/CN-catalyzed peroxymonosulfate (PMS) in p-nitrophenol degradation, we revealed that the CDs enriched with carboxyl groups or doped by pyrrolic-N inclined to attract photogenerated electrons from CN host, and thus contributed to the photocarriers separation. As such, these CDs-imparted active sites can dramatically facilitate PMS activation, and further p-nitrophenol degradation by photogenerated holes and multiple reactive oxygen radicals. Nonetheless, the CDs mainly doped by electron-donating graphitic-N could form electron-rich regions at the interface of CN host upon forming heterojunction, thus making barriers against charge separation in the PMS-mediated oxidation process. Taken together, this study will shed light on the rational design of CDs/CN heterojunctions with high photocatalytic activity.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In the context of realizing worldwide target of carbon neutrality, advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) have been projected as promising technologies to address environmental issues. Among them, developing sulfates radical-based AOPs by the activation of persulfate (i.e., peroxydisulfate (PDS) or peroxymonosulfate (PMS)) is of considerable interest to the scientific community, owing to the superiority in degradation of various refractory organic pollutants [1–3]. In terms of potential technological applications, photocatalyst-driven persulfate activation is more superior to other energy-consumed activation strategies (i.e., thermolysis, photolysis, radiolysis) based on the advantages of energy-saving feature, ease of operation, and mild reaction conditions [4]. Therefore, the synthesis of available and efficient catalysts is one of the most important links for engineering application of persulfate-mediated AOPs. The homogeneous metal-based catalysis is commonly advantageous to charge transfer between persulfate and photocatalysts, and consequently give fast reaction process, but the slow metal reduction and unsatisfactory retrievability severely limit its utilization in environmental remediation [5]. For this issue, research focus has been transferred to exploiting carbocatalysts with high chemical stability, tunable active sites and excellent electron migration property, such as carbon nitride (CN), graphene, carbon nanotubes and biochar [6–9]. Among these proposed catalysts, CN with unique photochemical activity holds great promise to establish AOPs by photocatalytic activation of PMS or PDS. However, CN in its pristine form commonly suffers from several drawbacks, such as limited visible light absorption, fast recombination rate of electron-hole pairs, low migration ability of photoexcited carriers and relatively few surface-active sites [10–12]. Therefore, enhancing the photocatalytic activity of CN through rational design of structure remains a significant scientific challenge.

To this end, different methodologies, including heteroatom doping, defect engineering, nanoarchitecture design, and heterojunction construction, have been developed [2,10,13,14]. As a novel type of carbon-based nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) are easily achievable, inexpensive, and nontoxic. Besides, these emerging 0D nanostructures have unique optical properties, superior electron transfer ability, and electron reservoir capacity, which enables the flexible design of carbonaceous catalysts [15,16]. Considering these attractive properties, tremendous efforts have thus been directed to constructing heterojunctions based on CDs and CN to improve the photocatalytic activity of CN host by extending the photoresponse range, optimizing exciton dissociation, or introducing more active sites [17]. Through deep research on CDs/CN heterojunctions, it has been found that CDs could behave as electron mediators, electron reservoirs, photosensitizers and spectral converters. More intriguingly, the incorporation of CDs is prone to rearrange original electron distribution at the interface of CN host, by which the photogenerated electrons or holes could serve as the dominant contributors or initiators in photocatalytic reactions [11,18]. As has been widely reported, optimizing the photoelectrical properties of CDs through nitrogen doping is favorable to introduce active sites within multicomponent photocatalysts [19–21]. Particularly, recent studies have revealed that implanting nitrogen-doped CDs in CN matrix is beneficial to photocarriers separation within heterojunctions, and thus substantially improving the photocatalytic performance of CN in persulfate-mediated AOPs [22,23].

For the hybridization of CDs and CN, the involved strategies mainly include physical mixture, ultrasonication, electronic assembly, hydrothermal/solvothermal, calcine, and so forth [16]. Among these developed strategies, most of previous researches prefer to strengthen the heterojunction between CDs and CN by calcinating the mixture of N-CDs and CN precursor at temperature above 500℃ [22,24–27], but doing this could inevitably lose the optical and electronic properties of CDs, because the CDs is more likely to form an integral structure with CN through complex reactions, rather than evolve as CDs-imparted active sites. Therefore, implanting CDs within CN structure by thermal copolymerization leads to the ambiguous cognition on the relationship between functional structure of CDs and enhanced photocatalytic activity of CDs/CN composite. More specifically, the contribution of CDs for the efficient photocarriers separation within heterojunction is hardly to be comprehend. In general, CDs physically loaded by CN matrix is a mild and facile strategy to construct heterojunction based on π–π stacking interaction. More importantly, this strategy can energetically integrate the attractive properties of both CDs and CN, allowing for the formation of CDs-imparted active sites on the surfaces of CN matrix. Inspired by such knowledge, we synthesized CDs with different functional structures by specific synthesis strategy, and then physically integrated them with CN to construct heterojunctions. Furthermore, to gain insight into regulatory effect of CDs in the photocatalyst-driven PMS activation process, we systematically investigated p-nitrophenol (PNP) degradation over the CDs/CN-catalyzed PMS activation systems. In depth, by analyzing the dependence of photocatalytic activity of composite catalysts on the functionalities of CDs, we attempted to elucidate the relationships among the functional structure of CDs, construction strategy of CDs/CN heterojunctions, and ultimate the photocatalytic performance.
All the chemicals used in this study were of analytical reagent grade. The detailed information can be found in the Supplementary Material, Text S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of different functionalized CDs samples
ce:section.ce:para: The first type of nitrogen-doped CDs (denoted as 1 N-CDs) were synthesized according to a reported method with minor modifications [28]. The typical synthesis procedure is depicted as follows: 1.94 g of citric acid monohydrate, 3.60 g of urea and 0.30 g of NH4F were mixed thoroughly by grinding. The obtained mixture was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and thermally treated at 200℃ for 2 h, giving the final solid 1 N-CDs sample. The CDs sample was dissolved in ultrapure water and then centrifugated to remove aggregated particles at a speed of 8,000 rpm for 10 min. For further purification, the 1 N-CDs solution was filtered through a PTFE syringe filter with pore size of 0.22 μm, and then subjected to dialysis (MWCO = 1,000 Da) against ultrapure water for 24 h. The other type of nitrogen-doped CDs (labeled as 2 N-CDs) was synthesized via a one-pot hydrothermal process. Specifically, 525 mg of citric acid monohydrate and 108 mg of m-phenylenediamine were dissolved in 10 mL of ultrapure water. Subsequently, the resulting homogeneous mixture was sealed in a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and hydrothermally treated at 180℃ for 8 h. After the reaction finished, the deep yellow 2 N-CDs solution was obtained and then the purification was carried out following the same procedure as used for 1 N-CDs. According to a previous report, CDs enriched with carboxyl groups was synthesized using only citric acid as precursor (here denoted as ca-CDs) [29], and the detailed synthesis procedure was depicted as follows: 100 g of citric acid monohydrate in a round-bottom flask was heated at 180℃ for 24 h. After cooling to room temperature, the obtained black solid was fully dissolved in 100 mL of NaOH solution (1 mol/L) and then the pH was adjusted to about 7 using NaOH solution. The obtained black ca-CDs solution was centrifuged at 8,000 rpm for 10 min, and finally purified in the same way as mentioned above. All the CDs samples were freeze-dried, and the concentrations were thus determined to be 8.39, 12.33, and 409.75 mg/mL for 1 N-, 2 N-, and ca-CDs, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of pristine CN and CDs/CN composites
ce:section.ce:para: The pristine CN was prepared by a simple calcination method that was reported extensively [11,25]. Typically, 3 g of dicyandiamide in a closed crucible was placed into a muffle furnace. Then, the pyrolytic temperature proceeded to increase with a ramping rate of 5℃/min to 550℃ and maintained for 4 h. After that, the as-prepared CN with light yellow color was milled into powder using an agate mortar. For the preparation of CDs/CN composites, 200 mg of CN powder was added into 20 mL of ethanol, and then the suspension was subjected to sonication treatment for better dispersion. The processing power and holding time were set as 150 W and 30 min, respectively. After sonication, 200 μL of purified 1 N-CDs solution was fully mixed with the above CN suspension by magnetic stirring for 30 min. Ultimately, the 1 N-CDs/CN composite was obtained by oven-drying at 60℃ overnight. The 2 N-CDs/CN and ca-CDs/CN composites were prepared in the same manner as the 1 N-CDs/CN composite, except that the added volume of 2 N-CDs and ca-CDs solutions were 136 and 4.1 μL, respectively, to ensure the same loading content of different CDs samples. The prepared CN was also treated by sonication and used as the control.
The specific information of materials characterization was shown in the Supplementary Material, Text S2.

PNP, which is an omnipresent hazardous organic pollutant in water resources, was selected to evaluate the photocatalytic activity of CN-based catalysts in batch experiments with PMS as an oxidant. A 300 W Xenon lamp was placed 30 cm away from the photocatalytic reactor and provided the sunlight source with an irradiation intensity of 150 mW/cm2. Before initiating the photocatalysis, 50 mg of catalyst was added into 40 mL of PNP solution (with a concentration of 10 mg/L), and then the mixture was continuously stirred in the dark for 30 min to establish the adsorption–desorption equilibrium. After that, 36.8 mg of PMS was promptly added, and meanwhile the Xenon lamp was turned on to initiate the photocatalytic reaction. At given time intervals, 3.4 mL of aqueous aliquot was withdrawn, and then the sample was filtered three times using 0.22 μm polyethersulfone syringe filter prior to analysis. The variation of PNP concentration was determined by UV–vis spectrophotometry at absorption wavelength of 317 nm. The catalytic experiment for each catalyst was conducted in triplicates, and the results obtained from batch tests were averaged.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical structure and optical properties of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: As observed from high-resolution transmission electron microscopic (HR-TEM) images, all the synthesized CDs samples were well dispersed nanoparticles and exhibited quasi-spherical shapes (Fig. 1 a–c). Moreover, HR-TEM images of 1 N-CDs and 2 N-CDs displayed prominent crystal fringes with an interplanar spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) in-plane lattice of graphene [30]. As for the ca-CDs, clear lattice structure with a crystal plane spacing of 0.19 nm was observed in the corresponding HR-TEM image, which can be assigned to the (103) crystal plane of graphite carbon [20]. Additionally, as presented in Fig. 1d, all the CDs samples exhibited narrow size distributions in the range of 1 ∼ 4 nm, and the average diameters were measured to be 2.5, 2.8, and 2.8 nm for 1 N-CDs, 2 N-CDs, and ca-CDs respectively. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of 1 N-CDs in Fig. 1e exhibited a relatively sharp peak at 26.4°, which was assigned to the graphite lattice spacing of (002) facet [31], this feature indicated that the 1 N-CDs was crystallized graphite phase. The XRD pattern of 2 N-CDs displayed a narrow diffraction peak centered at 19.1°, corresponding to an interlayer distance of about 0.47 nm [32], the slightly enlarged lattice spacing with respect to the (002) crystal plane of graphitic carbon (d = 0.34 nm) was more likely due to the inclusion of nitrogen atoms within sp2 flakes. In contrast, a broad diffraction peak at about 17.5° can be observed from ca-CDs, which was indicative of amorphous nature of carbonaceous cores. Taken together, the graphitization degree of these nanostructures was highly dependent on the precursor types and synthesis methods. Fourier transformed infrared (FTIR) spectrum of 1 N-CDs revealed the stretching vibrations for C−O, C−N, C=C/C=N, and C−H bonds at 1168, 1400, 1614, and 2948 cm−1, respectively (Fig. 1f). The distinct absorption peak located at 1666 cm−1 was assigned to C=O groups of amide Ⅰ band. Besides, the strong vibration absorption band at about 3440 cm−1 indicated the presence of abundant amino (N−H) and hydrogen (O−H) groups on 1 N-CDs surfaces [33,34]. Noticeably, although NH4F was used as dopant for the 1 N-CDs synthesis, it seems difficult to ascertain the C−F bond from FTIR spectrum. The FTIR spectrum of 2 N-CDs revealed the almost same chemical moieties with those contained in 1 N-CDs, stretching vibrations of C−O (1194 cm−1), C−N (1400 cm−1), C=C/C=N (1633 cm−1), C=O (1715 cm−1), C−H (2948 cm−1) and N−H/O−H (3440 cm−1) groups. For the ca-CDs without nitrogen doping, the FTIR absorption peaks located at 3440, 2948, 1633, and 1194 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of O−H, C−H, C=C and C−O, respectively. The characteristic peak with high intensity at 1715 cm−1 was originated from the stretching vibration of carboxylic groups on ca-CDs surfaces. Moreover, the bands at about 1394 and 1573 cm−1 were assigned to the characteristic absorption of symmetric and antisymmetric stretches of carboxylate ions [29].

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was conducted to gain insight into the surface chemical states of synthesized CDs. The XPS survey spectrum confirmed that the 1 N-CDs were mainly composed of C (65.2%, atomic percentage), N (12.1%, atomic percentage), and O (21.8%, atomic percentage) elements with binding energy of 285, 400, and 531 eV, attributable to C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s, respectively (Fig. S1a). It should be mentioned that only a trace amount of F element (0.9%) can be discerned, this is potentially because fluorine element containing in dopant was ionic form. Despite this, NH4F decomposition provided strong acid environment, which could facilitate the dehydration and carbonization of precursors. Additionally, the ammonia generated from NH4F decomposition also contributed to nitrogen doping in some extent. The full XPS spectrum of 2 N-CDs revealed their primary elemental composition of C (69.9%), N (6.2%), and O (23.9%) (Fig. S1b). In the case of ca-CDs, the XPS survey spectrum disclosed the basic composition of C (71.4%), Na (3.5%), and O (25.1%) elements (Fig. S1c), in which the trace amount of Na was from residual NaOH. According to the high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1 s (Fig. 2 a and d), 1 N-CDs and 2 N-CDs demonstrated the same carbon-related chemical components, including C=C/C−C (284.8 eV), C−N (285.2 eV), C−O (286.6 eV), and O=C−O (288.3 eV) bonds [33,34]. Because of being synthesized without nitrogen doping, the C 1 s band of ca-CDs showed three typical peaks at 284.8, 286.5 and 288.5 eV, attributed to C=C/C−C, C−O, and O=C−O bonds, respectively (Fig. 2g). As can be seen in Fig. 2b, e, and h, the high-resolution O 1 s spectra of all CDs samples revealed the same two components, those are C=O at about 531.5 eV and C−O at about 533.1 eV [35,36]. According to the high-resolution XPS spectra of N 1 s, none of N-related signals was observed from ca-CDs detection because the precursor did not contain nitrogen dopant (Fig. 2i). For the nitrogen-doped CDs (Fig. 2c and f), the N 1 s band of 1 N-CDs can be deconvoluted into two peaks corresponding to pyrrolic N (399.5 eV) and amino N (401.7 eV), while the 2 N-CDs revealed the existence of graphitic N (400.1 eV) and amino N (401.7 eV) [36,37]. Furthermore, the nitrogen species within 1 N-CDs structure was mainly in the form of pyrrolic N (87.3% of the total N signal), while for the 2 N-CDs, the graphitic N (81.7% of the total N signal) was the primary nitrogen-related chemical moieties. Recent research has performed an in-depth study on the mode of nitrogen incorporation during the CDs synthesis, the researchers pointed out that the inclusion modes of nitrogen atoms within CDs structures were highly dependent on the dosage of nitrogen dopant. Specifically, for relative lower dopant, the nitrogen atoms incorporated into the sp2 domains were mainly in the forms of graphitic-N. As dramatically increasing the dosage, nitrogen atoms mainly bonded at the edges of aromatic domains in the pyrrolic positions [38]. In our case, for the synthesis system of 1 N-CDs containing citric acid, urea, and NH4F, the mass ratio of nitrogen dopant to carbon source reached 2:1, which was comparable to the above-mentioned literature. Therefore, in the thermal polymerization of precursors, non-aromatic sp3-hybridized intermediates suffered difficulty to generate poly-aromatic structures containing graphitic nitrogen atoms, the nitrogen atoms could be mainly in the forms of pyrrolic configuration. Unlike the synthesis system of 1 N-CDs, the mass ratio of nitrogen dopant to carbon source was 1:5 in the starting materials of 2 N-CDs. Thus, due to the superfluous carbon source, the condensation reaction between the acid and the amine (m-phenylenediamine) could easily occurred in the initial stage of 2 N-CDs formation and then followed by carbonization to produce sp2 domains. In such case, the further fusion of the aromatic hydrocarbon domains resulted in a substantial contribution of the graphitic nitrogen atoms.

As revealed in Fig. 3 , the 1 N-CDs, 2 N-CDs, and ca-CDs demonstrated excitation-dependent photoluminescence (PL) behaviors with maximum emission peaks located at 450, 466, and 456 nm, respectively. The optical absorption behavior of all the CDs samples displayed two typical peaks in UV region, with the former assigned to π–π* transition of aromatic sp2-domains in carbon cores, while the absorption behavior in longer wavelength region was originated from n–π* transition of surface-contained C=O or C=N groups [39]. Note that the N-CDs exhibited a quite strong π–π* transition absorption, which might be associated with efficient electron transfer within sp2-domains. By comparison, the 1 N-CDs demonstrated more distinct electron transition from n-orbital to π* orbital, indicating the presence of abundant electron-donating moieties (resulting in the formation of n-orbital) at the edge sites of sp2 basal plane. Furthermore, for each CDs sample, the excitation spectrum was well overlapped with the absorption peak in longer wavelength region, implying that PL emission mainly originated from defect/surface state rather than quantum confinement effect [40–43]. Based on these experimental observations, the surface functionalities could have huge effect on photoelectron dynamics of CDs. The fluorescence quantum yields of all CDs samples were measured by using quinine sulfate as reference substance, showing the results of 24.3% for 1 N-CDs, 52.0% for 2 N-CDs, and 4.9% for ca-CDs. In general, the quantum yield of CDs is highly dependent on radiative and non-radiative recombination rates, in which the decreased radiative recombination rates could be partially attributed to charge carrier separation at the surfaces of CDs [33,44]. As confirmed by many previous reports on nitrogen-doped CDs, the inclusion of graphitic N in aromatic domains can largely increase the electron density of conjugated aromatic π systems by providing delocalized electron. Also, such structural configuration tends to enhance the rigidity of the sp2 flakes, and thus substantially reduces the probability of non-radiative relaxation through vibrational modes of the molecules [33]. Owing to the contribution from graphitic N to the electronic conjugate structure, the 2 N-CDs could greatly prevail over the other two CDs samples in radiative recombination rate, thus showing the ultrahigh quantum yield. By contrast, it has been extensively reported that the surface defects created by surface oxidation (i.e. surface-contained carbonyl and carboxyl groups) can serve as capture centers for excitons [33,42,44,45]. In such case, there could be a high probability of nonradiative recombination occurred on ca-CDs surfaces, thus resulting in lower quantum yield.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and chemical structure of CDs/CN hybrids
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology and microstructure of composite materials were investigated by field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM), as displayed in Fig. S2, all the CN-based composites possessed sheet-like structures. Owing to the nano-sized characteristic of CDs, the presence of CDs at the surfaces of CN matrix can be hardly observed from the SEM images of composites. The TEM images in Fig. 4 a–d clearly show that the CN matrix consisted of several stacking layers with different size of nanosheets, which could provide numerous anchor sites for CDs. Previous research has reported that CDs/CN composite with high catalytic performance can be in-situ prepared by sonication treatment of CN matrix [46], but in this work, the pristine CN sample was maintained in its raw state after sonication treatment due to the low ultrasonic power and short treatment period. The HR-TEM images illustrated that lots of nanosized CDs were uniformly distributed on the CN surfaces (marked by yellow circles), which could imply the formation of micro-regional heterojunctions based on CDs and CN matrix. This observation was in accordance with previous researches that CDs can be uniformly and tightly attached on the CN surface based on π–π stacking interaction [16,47]. With the precondition of intact CDs’ functional structures, the strong junction between CDs and CN could be beneficial to electron transfer across CDs/CN interface. Also, the ultrafine size of CDs was conducive to shortening the migration pathway of photo-induced carriers, and thus brought benefit to consecutive reaction. As shown in Fig. 4e, powder XRD pattern of the pristine CN showed two pronounced peaks at 2θ values of 12.9° and 27.3°, which were indexed to the (100) inplane packing and (002) interlayer stacking, respectively [48,49]. Due to the low content of CDs on CN surface, the XRD patterns of all CDs/CN composites only revealed typical characteristic of CN. The above result was largely different from the reported CDs/CN heterojunctions constructed through thermal polymerization, by which the crystal lattice of CN subjected to distortion [24]. As such, it can be deduced that the heterojunctions constructed in our research was formed by π–π stacking interaction between the two nanomaterials. Room-temperature electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectra was conducted to gain insight into electronic structural changes induced by CDs incorporation, as shown in Fig. 4f. Although the g-value of all samples showed almost no changes, the signals of 1 N-CDs/CN and ca-CDs/CN were considerably enhanced compared with pristine CN and 2 N-CDs/CN, suggesting the higher amount of free charge carriers in the formed heterojunctions [46,50,51]. This, in turn, revealed the successful construction of CDs/CN heterojunctions by the simple physical integration. Fig. S3 presents FTIR spectra of CDs/CN composites, the broad peak ranging from 3000 to 3500 cm−1 was attributed to the N−H groups, originating from the incomplete condensation or the residual hydrogen atoms bound to the edges of graphitic-like CN. The s-triazine ring mode at 808 cm−1 and stretching vibration modes in the region of 1240–1640 cm−1 belonged to C=N and C−N heterocycles [10]. Significantly, the characteristic FTIR peaks belonging to CDs was hardly detected in CDs/CN composites due to their body structure of CN matrix and low content of CDs.

The surface composition of CN and its composite counterparts was investigated by XPS analysis. As shown in Fig. S4, the XPS spectra in the full range demonstrated that all the samples were mainly composed of carbon and nitrogen with a small amount of oxygen. It is worth noting that the CN/CDs composites demonstrated increased oxygen content compared to pristine CN, which was attributed to the abundant oxygen-related groups on CDs surfaces. Elemental analysis was further conducted to confirm these structural changes. Specifically, the oxygen content of pristine CN was 1.30 ± 0.11%, while the 1 N-CDs/CN, 2 N-CDs/CN, and ca-CDs/CN demonstrated 4.53 ± 0.11%, 5.74 ± 0.09%, and 9.09 ± 0.11% of oxygen content, respectively. The increased oxygen content further validated the successful incorporation of CDs in CN matrix by physical mixture approach. As shown in the high-resolution XPS spectra in Fig. S5, all the CDs/CN composites revealed the same chemical moieties as those contained in pristine CN. Specifically, the C 1 s band could be deconvoluted into three peaks, including 284.8 eV for graphitic carbon (C−C/C=C), 286.2 eV for carbon atoms in C−O species, and 288.3 eV for sp2-bonded carbon (N=C−N) in the s-triazine rings, respectively [10,13]. Moreover, the N 1 s spectrum can be fitted with three components with peaks at 398.8, 400.3, and 401.3 eV, respectively, corresponding to sp2-hybridized nitrogen in the triazine ring (C−N=C), tertiary nitrogen (N−(C)3), and amine groups (−NH2/NH) at the edge of heptazine framework, respectively. The weak peak located at 404.5 eV could be attributed to the positive charge localization in heterocycles [14,52,53]. To further elucidate the formation process of CDs/CN composites, the variations of surface charge were measured, and the results were given in Fig. 4g. The Zeta potential of pristine CN was − 3.52 mV, consistent with the previous report [41]. In contrast, the 1 N-CDs/CN and 2 N-CDs/CN exhibited positive potential of 7.32 and 3.95 mV, respectively, implying the strong electronic coupling between 1 N-CDs or 2 N-CDs and CN matrix in addition to the π–π stacking interaction. Compared to the pristine CN, the surface of ca-CDs/CN composite was more negatively charged, with Zeta potential value of − 11.13 mV, it is inferred that the ca-CDs enriched with carboxyl was negatively charged and thus contributed to this variation. Moreover, N2 adsorption–desorption isotherm was employed to measure the specific surface area and pore structure of the CDs/CN composites. As exhibited in Fig. S6 and S7, all the samples had type Ⅳ isotherm with H-type hysteresis loop and a well-defined mesopore distribution. The specific surface areas of pristine CN, 1 N-CDs/CN, 2 N-CDs/CN, and ca-CDs/CN were determined to be 8.77, 6.77, 7.51, and 6.94 m2/g, respectively. The possible reason for the slight decrease in surface area was that the mesoporous of CN host could be partially occluded by nano-sized CDs. Taken together, the photocatalytic activity of composite catalysts was independent of specific surface area, which also indicated that the functional structure of CDs rather than the distribution density of CDs-imparted active sites was the decisive factor for tailoring photocatalytic activity of CDs/CN composite catalysts. Furthermore, the thermal stability of CDs/CN composites was studied by thermogravimetric analysis, as shown in Fig. S8. Compared with pristine CN, all the CDs/CN composites showed slightly higher weight loss in the temperature range of 300 to 650℃, which could be mainly attributed to the decomposition of CDs.

The absorption behavior of CDs/CN composites was investigated by diffused reflectance spectra (DRS). As seen in Fig. 5 a, the bulk CN showed photoabsorption from UV light to visible light, with an absorption edge at 440 nm. Specifically, the absorption edge in DRS of 2 N-CDs/CN and ca-CDs/CN exhibited inapparent red-shift toward visible light region, presumably because the two types of functionalized CDs cannot harvest photons with wavelength beyond 460 nm. In contrast, the DRS of 1 N-CDs/CN composite displayed an evident increase in the tail absorbance and red-shift of the adsorption edge, which was probably due to the photoabsorption by interfacial states of 1 N-CDs/CN [11]. The band gap of a semiconductor can be estimated from Tauc plot [i.e., curve of converted (αhν)2 versus hν from the UV–vis spectrum, in which α, h, and ν are the absorption coefficient, Planck constant, and light frequency, respectively, and a constant of 2 is applied for a direct band gap material]. As can be seen from Fig. 5b, following the loading of CDs, the band gap of CN host was slightly narrowed, with value ranging from 2.78 to 2.75 eV. This indicates that the three types of functionalized CDs had little effect on the bandgap and bandgap positions of CN host. In this regard, the surface plasmon resonance effect should be responsible for the extended visible-light absorption of 1 N-CDs/CN composite [46]. Fig. 5c displays the fluorescence emission spectra of pristine CN and CDs/CN composites at the excitation wavelength of 380 nm. The pristine CN showed a strong emission peak at 460 nm, which was originated from the band-to-band recombination of the electrons and holes [54]. Intriguingly, the fluorescence intensity of 2 N-CDs/CN was clearly higher than that of pristine CN, we deduce that the 2 N-CDs with ultrahigh quantum yield could serve as electron-efficient centers, by which the recombination of electron-hole pairs within heterojunction was greatly promoted. As expected, both the 1 N-CDs/CN and ca-CDs/CN presented relative low fluorescence intensities compared to that of pristine CN, which is indicative of electron transfer between CN and CDs. This quenching appears to be related to the electron trapping occurred on the surfaces of 1 N-CDs or ca-CDs, therefore, upon loading with 1 N-CDs and ca-CDs, the CN host achieved a higher availability of holes at the valence band. The time-resolved fluorescence decay spectra in Fig. 5d further supported the above-mentioned analysis, as much slower exponential decay dynamics was observed from 1 N-CDs/CN and ca-CDs/CN composites. Through tri-exponential function fitting, the average lifetimes were determined. As a result, incorporating 1 N-CDs and ca-CDs prolonged the average lifetime of CN from 6.72 to 9.05 and 7.43 ns, respectively. This change regularity revealed that the 1 N-CDs and ca-CDs could serve as trapping sites for photogenerated carriers, and thus being favorable for charge separation within heterojunctions. In contrast, the 2 N-CDs incorporation can hardly prolong fluorescence lifetime of CN host. To further reveal the photoelectronic characteristics of the as-prepared composite catalysts, the photocurrent response was measured. Fig. 5e shows the transient photocurrent response of CDs/CN composites with the repeated on–off cycles under intermittent illumination. Among these CN-based catalysts, the 1 N-CDs/CN exhibited the highest photocurrent intensity under sunlight irradiation, which was more than two-times higher than that of pristine CN. Similarly, the ca-CDs/CN also revealed strong photocurrent response compared to that of pristine CN, while the 2 N-CDs/CN showed minor difference with pristine CN in photocurrent intensity. The effects of different CDs modifications on the charge transfer resistance of CN were further disclosed via electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) (Fig. 5f). The smaller semicircle radius is, the lower charge transfer resistance, thus, the 1 N-CDs prevailed over 2 N-CDs in terms of promoting charge separation in CN. Notably, the heterojunction constructed by incorporating ca-CDs on CN matrix also afforded a remarkable ability of charge separation as compared to pristine CN, while the 2 N-CDs loaded by CN matrix can hardly accelerate the photocarriers migration within the formed heterojunction. According to the Mott-Schottky analysis of pristine CN and CDs/CN composites (Fig. S9), all the samples were characterized by n-type conductivity [55], indicating that the semiconductor of CN did not alter upon decoration of CDs via the physical integration. The flat-band potential (Efb) was determined by extrapolating the linear part of Mott-Schottky plots to the x intercept with the integration of CDs, Efb of CN matrix was positively shifted from − 1.14 to − 0.93 V. Such slight anodic shift could be related to the fact that the 1 N-CDs, 2 N-CDs, and ca-CDs samples possessed different potential regimes [19,56]. Importantly, from the Mott-Schottky plots, it can be seen that 1 N-CDs/CN and ca-CDs/CN composites presented smaller slopes compared to pristine CN, while the slope obtained from 2 N-CDs/CN slightly larger than that determined from pristine CN. This, in turn, suggested an increase of donor density in the 1 N-CDs/CN and ca-CDs/CN composites, which was attributed to the improvement of electrical conductivity and charge transport [57]. Based on the above-mentioned analysis, the deposition of 1 N-CDs or ca-CDs on to bulk CN was likely to introduce electron/hole trapping sites within the electronic structure of CN, because CDs with multiple functional structures were typically characterized by numerous electronic localized states, these emerging electronic states in heterojunctions could easily change the charge distribution and photocarriers migration behavior at CN interface, thus providing large possibilities for efficient utilization of photogenerated charge carriers in photocatalytic systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic activity of CN/CDs toward PMS activation
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic activities of CDs/CN composites toward PMS activation were evaluated by PNP degradation efficiency. Note that the degradation efficiency was represented by C t/C 0, where C 0 and C t represent PNP concentrations at time t = 0 and t, respectively. Before starting the reaction, the mixture of the catalyst and PNP solution was stirred in the dark for 30 min to achieve adsorption–desorption equilibrium. As shown in Fig. 6 a, taking the 1 N-CDs/CN composite as a representative catalyst sample for comparison, PNP was barely degraded by sunlight irradiation or PMS alone; Also, when the experiment was carried out in the absence of photocatalyst, the degradation efficiency of PNP was only 3.7% in the PMS + sunlight system, indicating that the PMS was ineffective in generating •OH and SO4 •− radicals without activation. Moreover, PNP degradation was negligible in the coupling system of 1 N-CDs/CN and PMS without sunlight irradiation, implying that photocarriers generation is a decisive factor for PMS activation. Comparatively, 1 N-CDs/CN composite with both light irradiation and PMS addition degraded 95.9% of PNP in the reaction period of 35 min. The photocatalytic efficiency of catalyst alone was measured under sunlight irradiation, as shown in Fig. 6b. In the catalytic reaction system without PMS, only 3.4%, 9.6%, 3.8%, and 6.2% of PNP removal efficiencies were afforded by pristine CN, 1 N-CDs/CN, 2 N-CDs/CN, and ca-CDs/CN, respectively. Therefore, the degradation of PNP in CDs/CN + PMS + light system relied on photocatalyst-driven PMS activation. Incidentally, compared to the PNP removal catalyzed by pristine CN, the degradation efficiencies of PNP afforded by 1 N-CDs/CN and ca-CDs/CN exhibited almost 3 and 2-fold increase, respectively. Fig. 6c depicts PNP degradation efficiencies for different photocatalyzed PMS activation systems. It would appear that the 1 N-CDs were available to activate PMS, wherein the degradation of PNP was 22.1%. In contrast, the 2 N-CDs and ca-CDs were insufficient to promote PMS activation, the percentage degradation of PNP were only 6.4% and 3.7%, respectively. Regarding to the CN-based catalysts, the PNP removal rate was 29.3% in the CN + PMS + light system in the period of photocatalytic reaction, indicating that CN had insufficient catalytic sites for PMS activation. As previously reported, the random transfer of photocarriers in CN matrix, arising from intrinsic graphitic sp2-hybridized array of tri-s-triazine repeating units and inert stack of layers, hinder photocarriers separation and thus give rise to unsatisfactory catalytic performance [14]. Unlike the 1 N-CDs/CN-catalyzed PMS activation process (showing 95.9% of PNP removal rate), decorating CN with 2 N-CDs was detrimental to photocatalytic activity of CN host, as seen, only 21.1% of PNP was degraded in the 2 N-CDs/CN + PMS + light system within 35 min. This distinct phenomenon suggested that the contribution to CN catalytic performance from nitrogen-doped CDs was highly dependent on their specific N-related chemical moieties. Intriguingly, although the ca-CDs was free from nitrogen doping, the constructed ca-CDs/CN heterojunction contributed to 68.7% of PNP degradation in the PMS-mediated AOPs, further manifesting that the functional structures of CDs were nonnegligible for the formation of high-performance heterojunction. The kinetics was calculated using a pseudo-first-order kinetic model: −ln (C t/C 0) = kt, where k is the reaction rate constant. As shown in Fig. 6d, PNP degradation kinetics demonstrated the largest rate constant in the 1 N-CDs/CN-catalyzed PMS activation system, with a value of 0.088 min−1. For the ca-CDs/CN + PMS + light system, the k value decreased to 0.033 min−1, but was still higher than that of the pristine CN + PMS + light system by 3.3-fold. Among all, the 2 N-CDs/CN + PMS + light system displayed the lowest reaction rate with k value of 0.007 min−1, confirming that the 2 N-CDs was ineffective to boost up the catalytic activity of CN. To evaluate the reusability of the 1 N-CDs/CN and ca-CDs/CN, the degradation of PNP was carried out in four successive cycles, as illustrated in Fig. S10. The two photocatalysts showed excellent reusability in the PMS-mediated oxidation systems, displaying only 11.5% and 0.7% loss in catalytic activity, respectively. For better understanding the effectiveness of CDs/CN heterojunctions constructed in our research, we compared different CN-based carbocatalysts utilized in sulfates radical-based AOPs, and the result was summarized in Table S1.

To identify the reactive species in CDs/CN-catalyzed PMS activation process, radical quenching analysis was performed by treating •OH with tertiary butyl alcohol (t-BA), O2 •− with 4-chloro-7-nitrobenzofurazan (NBD-Cl), 1O2 with β-carotene, h+ with Na2C2O4, respectively. Moreover, MeOH was used as an effective quenching agent for both •OH and SO4 •−, and silver nitrate (SN) was used as electron sacrificial agent [22,58,59]. As shown in Fig. 6e, in the 1 N-CDs/CN + PMS + light system, incorporating MeOH and t-BA led to the decrease of PNP removal rate from 95.9% to 48.0% and 40.6%, respectively. The negligible difference between MeOH and t-BA in inhibitory effects revealed that the SO4 •− in bulk solution was not the primary reactive oxygen species for PNP degradation. As seen, the PNP removal rate decreased from 95.9% to 52.3% with the addition of SN, indicating that the photogenerated electrons could be the initiators for generating active species. Furthermore, we found that Na2C2O4, as an efficient scavenger of h+, had the largest inhibitory effect, resulting in that only 23.7% of the PNP was removed. As a typical non-radical oxidation pathway, the contribution of 1O2 for PNP removal was secondary to the photogenerated h+, because the 1 N-CDs/CN-catalyzed PMS activation system merely degraded 32.1% of PNP in the presence of β-carotene. Such phenomenon was also observed in previous reports, that is non-radical pathway dominated by singlet oxygen (1O2) existed in non-metallic carbon catalytic activation process of persulfate [60,61]. Although the inhibitory effect of NBD-Cl was weaker than that of other quenching agents, the existence of this quenching agent also led to the remarkable decrease of PNP removal rate from 95.9% to 67.2%. Taken together, the roles of those reactive species involved in PNP degradation process followed the order h+ >1O2 > •OH > O2 •− > SO4 •−. As for the ca-CDs/CN + PMS + light system (Fig. 6f), the results about radical quenching also demonstrated that the contribution levels of reactive species followed the same sequence, which in turn confirmed that the deposition of 1 N-CDs and ca-CDs onto CN matrix could evolve as similar photocatalytic active sites. By comparing several previous studies focusing on CDs/CN-catalyzed PMS activation processes (as illustrated in Table S2), we found that the synthesis strategy for CDs/CN heterojunctions could largely affect the contribution levels of reactive species in the sulfates radical-based AOPs [22,23].

According to the above-mentioned analysis, the functional structure of CDs should play a key role in the formation of CDs-imparted active sites when the heterojunction was constructed based on π–π stacking interaction. To verify this hypothesis, the 1 N-CDs/CN and ca-CDs/CN were thermally treated at a temperature of 300℃ for 2 h under N2 atmosphere, the resultant samples were referred as 1 N-CDs/CN300 and ca-CDs/CN300, respectively. The thermal treatment deteriorated the photocatalytic activity of CDs/CN composites, resulting in 48.9% and 25.3% decrease of PNP removal efficiency in 1 N-CDs/CN– and ca-CDs/CN-catalyzed PMS activation processes (Fig. S11). Taking all these results together, we deduced that the structural variation of 1 N-CDs and ca-CDs caused by thermal treatment could be responsible for the decreased photocatalytic activity. As verified by FTIR analysis (Fig. 7 a and b), apart from the possible loss of surface adsorbed H2O molecular, it seems that the thermal treatment can hardly damage the frameworks of both 1 N-CDs/CN and ca-CDs/CN. Nonetheless, as revealed by the FTIR spectra in Fig. 7c and d, the chemical moieties of 1 N-CDs and ca-CDs were subjected to great changes after thermal treatment (the resultant samples were denoted as 1 N-CDs300 and ca-CDs300). Specifically, hydroxy and carboxyl groups attached on 1 N-CDs surface were partially vanished after the low-temperature calcination. Moreover, the N-related chemical species (N−H and C−N) also went through destruction. In the same vein, the thermal treatment led to the serious change of O-related chemical groups on ca-CDs surfaces (i.e. O−H, C=O, and C−O), suggesting the denaturation of CDs nanostructures. Therefore, the 1 N-CDs or ca-CDs loaded by CN host inevitably lost its function upon thermal treatment, which were responsible for the inferior photocatalytic activity of 1 N-CDs/CN300 and ca-CDs/CN300. In this sense, the functional structure of CDs played a decisive role in constructing the CDs/CN heterojunction with high-catalytic performance.

Previous studies have revealed that O2 •− can be not only generated from the reaction of dissolved oxygen and photo-induced electrons, but also derived from SO4 •− oxidation. It should be mentioned that some part of SO4 •− radicals that attached on the surface of catalyst can hardly degrade PNP directly, but these reactive species could facilitate the generation of O2 •− [22]. As such, we ascertained the possible presence of adsorbed SO4 •− as well as its influence on the generation of O2 •−. As shown in Fig. S12a, the experimental apparatus was reinstalled, by which the photocatalytic system can be degassed with N2. In this case, we found that both the 1 N-CDs/CN– and ca/CDs/CN-catalyzed PMS activation demonstrated severely limited oxidation ability as filling N2 into the bulk solution, resulting in the 31.0% and 20.1% decrease of PNP removal rate. Further, the co-existence of NBD-Cl and N2 in the reaction system exhibited comparable inhibitory effect to N2 environment (Fig. S12b and c). The above results not only revealed that O2 •− was derived from the reaction of dissolved O2 molecules with the photogenerated electrons, but also suggested the absence of surface adsorbed SO4 •−. As confirmed by previous reports, O2 molecules prefer to adsorb on electron-efficient regions, and further contribute to O2 •− production by receiving more electrons [62]. In such case, it is reasonable to deduce that the heterojunctions with high catalytic performance were attributed to the numerous electron-efficient centers induced by 1 N-CDs or ca-CDs. The strong inhibitory effect of photo-induced electron scavenger agent (SN) on PNP degradation efficiency also confirmed the above point. Therefore, incorporating 1 N-CDs or ca-CDs into CN matrix could induce the redistribution of charge carriers, as a result, the formed charge localization centers facilitated the electron transfer between composite catalysts and PMS oxidant.

The reactive oxygen species generated from 1 N-CDs/CN– and ca-CDs/CN-catalyzed PMS activation was further validated by EPR using 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO) and 2,2,6,6-tetramethyl-4-piperidinol (TEMP) as the radical trapping agents. As depicted in Fig. 8 , there were no characteristic DMPO-•OH/SO4 •−, DMPO-O2 •−, and TEMP-1O2 adducts signals when the PMS-mediated oxidation systems were in dark environment, indicating that the generation of radicals depended on photocatalytic reaction. Upon the simulated sunlight irradiation, the 1 N-CDs/CN– and ca-CDs/CN-catalyzed PMS activation displayed the almost same EPR signals of reactive oxygen species. Specifically, both the photocatalyst-driven PMS activation processes presented the distinct peaks assigned to the DMPO-•OH and weak peaks corresponding to the DMPO-SO4 •−. This result confirms that •OH was selectively produced in the PMS activation, and the PNP degradation did not rely on SO4 •− in bulk solution, which was consistent with the results of the scavenging experiments. As seen, the DMPO-O2 •− adduct with intense signals were clearly detected in EPR spectra after 10 min reaction, revealing the important part of O2 •− for PNP degradation. Moreover, the remarkable peak intensity of TEMP-1O2 signals can be also observed from EPR spectra, this result, combined with radical quenching analysis, forcefully demonstrated that the non-radical pathway (1O2) was predominant in 1 N-CDs/CN and ca-CDs/CN catalyzed PMS oxidation systems.

In the light of above analysis, both the radical pathway and non-radical pathway were involved in the 1 N-CDs/CN– and ca-CDs/CN-catalyzed PMS activation processes. Taking into consideration that the reactive oxygen species as well as their contribution levels were almost same, the production pathways of reactive species should be identical in the two PMS-mediated oxidation systems. Specifically, PMS was molecularly adsorbed on the composite catalysts, and the photo-induced electron transfer led to the PMS dissociation with the generation of •OH and SO4 •− radicals. As revealed by previous study, the generated SO4 •− radicals can be easily inactivated by the carboxyl groups on surfaces of carbocatalysts [63]. As such, the presence of carboxyl groups on the surfaces of 1 N-CDs and ca-CDs as revealed by FTIR and XPS may be a reason for the very few SO4 •− radicals in the reaction bulk solution. Simultaneously, O2 molecules adsorbed on electron-efficient regions resulted in the generation of abundant O2 •− by receiving more electrons. Furthermore, O2 •− was prone to react with H2O and H+, both the two processes contributed to the production of 1O2 and H2O2, in which H2O2 further dissociated into •OH. More importantly, the redistribution of photo-induced electrons as well as their targeted migration in heterojunctions led to the enrichment of photogenerated holes at the valence band of CN, by this way, the photogenerated holes played a major role in the photocatalytic systems. Based on above discussions, the possible reaction routes of photocatalytic processes have been described as Eqs. (1)−(6):

More recently, several important researches have designed high-performance heterojunction based on CN and CDs by thermal polymerization, and attributed the enhanced photocatalytic activity to the extended light absorption range and/or increased charge separation efficiency imparted by CDs [24–27]. Turning to the formation of the composite structure, CDs could react with CN or its molecular precursors during the thermal polymerization process, resulting in the changes of ordered structures within the CN framework [64]. Meanwhile, the functional groups of CDs could be easily destructed in the synthesis of composites. In contrast, the formation of CDs/CN heterojunction in our research was mainly attributed to π–π stacking interaction, which can inherit the physicochemical properties of CDs and maintain integrity of CN conjugated structure. To illustrate the dependence of catalytic performance of CDs/CN heterojunction on synthesis strategies, we operated the in-situ preparation of CDs/CN composites by directly calcinating the mixture of dicyandiamide and different CDs samples at 550℃ (the obtained composite sample was labeled as CDs/CN550), in which, the loading amount of CDs was the same as that in CDs/CN composites. As illustrated in Fig. S13, with PMS as additive oxidant, the photocatalytic degradation of PNP imparted by the 1 N-CDs/CN550, 2 N-CDs/CN550, and ca-CDs/CN550 were 14.3%, 9.4%, and 14.0%, respectively. Therefore, it appears that all the types of CDs were unfavorable to form high-performance heterojunction with CN matrix. Particularly for the 1 N-CDs/CN550 and ca-CDs/CN550, the photocatalytic performance was significantly inferior to that of pristine CN (29.3%), not to mention the comparability to their counterparts synthesized based on π–π stacking interaction. We conjecture that covalent bonds can be easily formed between CDs and polymer intermediates of CN in the high-temperature calcine process, accompanied by the decomposition of chemical moieties on CDs surfaces. In such case, it is more likely that the nanostructured CDs were implanted into the CN matrix at the expense of their optical and electronic properties. Therefore, the structure changes of CDs could be the primary reason for the inferior photocatalytic activity of CDs/CN550. In this regard, although some of high-performance heterojunctions based on CDs and CN can be designed by thermal copolymerization, the resultant high photocatalytic activity cannot simply be put down to the textural characteristics of CDs.

According to above results, the photocatalytic activity of CDs/CN heterojunction was highly dependent on the decisive process of efficient photocarriers separation and well-steered transfer of photo-induced electrons, because both the CDs and the pristine CN were inert or inferior toward PMS activation. In this case, there should be some underlying reasons traced to the roles of CDs’ functional structures for the well-steered electron migration within the heterostructure. As revealed by structural characterizations, the carbonaceous structures of all CDs samples demonstrated different graphitization degrees. Despite these differences, both the 1 N-CDs and ca-CDs can induce the formation of active sites upon anchoring on the surface of CN matrix. Thus, the dimension of sp2 domains was not the key factor for tailoring the electronic configuration of hybrid structure formed between CDs and CN. Turning to the chemical composition, it is apparent that the promoting effect of nitrogen-doped CDs on photocatalytic performance of heterojunction not only associated with the construction strategy of heterojunction, but also depended on inclusion mode of nitrogen within CDs structures, because the 1 N-CDs can dramatically enhance the photocatalytic of CN host, while the 2 N-CDs behaved the opposite way. As has been widely reported, the surface chemical moieties of CDs commonly have a decisive effect on their electronic configurations and further manipulate electron transfer behavior [19,38], thus there is always a trade-off between emissive and photocatalytic properties. In our case, the graphitic-N was the primary heteroatom doping species within 2 N-CDs, which can largely increase the electron density of sp2 flakes due to the additional π* electrons. Such sp2-domains had very few defects that might act as charge carriers traps, in line with the extremely high quantum yield. It has been experimentally confirmed that the chemical structures, especially the surface functional groups of CDs, were intact after combining with CN through π–π stacking interaction. Thereby, the electronic conjugate structure of 2 N-CDs could largely increase the electron density within the heterojunction of 2 N-CDs/CN due to the inclusion of graphitic-N, these emerging electron-efficient centers made barriers against charge separation during photocatalytic reaction. Unlike the 2 N-CDs modification, we conjectured that the 1 N-CDs and ca-CDs can provide high photocarriers transfer kinetics to drive electron-hole pair spatial separation within heterojunctions. As revealed by the lower quantum yield, ca-CDs with abundant surface carboxyl could possess nonradiative excited-state relaxation processes, namely surface defect-trapped carriers. These nonradiative recombination centers or defects on the surface of ca-CDs could be imparted to ca-CDs/CN heterojunction when the π-electron system of CN was strongly coupled with surface electronic states of ca-CDs. By this way, ca-CDs was more likely to extract photogenerated electrons from CN, and resulted in the accumulation of holes at the interface of CN host. Such unique electronic configuration gave a good explanation to the overwhelming superiority of photogenerated holes in the ca-CDs/CN-catalyzed PMS activation process. For the 1 N-CDs, the pyrrolic-N with higher electron affinity than the neighboring carbon atoms can strongly attract the π-electrons toward the edge site of carbon plane, enabling efficient interfacial electron transfer, namely surface emissive traps. As such, the capacity of 1 N-CDs to act as electron sinks could be the main driving force for the efficient photocarriers separation within 1 N-CDs/CN heterojunctions. Simultaneously, the well-steered electron migration left behind photogenerated holes at the valence band of CN matrix, which therefore contributed greatly to PNP degradation in PMS-mediated AOPs. Overall, for the 1 N-CDs/CN and ca-CDs/CN-catalyzed PMS activation, photo-induced electrons were promoted from the valence band to the conduction band of CN host and then transferred to 1 N-CDs and ca-CDs (as shown in Fig. 9 ). In this case, the high availability of holes at the valence band of CN contributed greatly to PNP degradation. Meanwhile, the facile electron transfer from CN to 1 N-CDs or ca-CDs gave rise to a phenomenon that PMS can be efficiently activated to generate reactive radicals by electron gain process.

In summary, the different types of functionalized CDs were synthesized, and further loaded on CN matrix for constructing heterojunctions with the retention of CDs’ functional structures. The photocatalytic activity of CDs/CN heterojunctions toward PMS-mediated AOPs was found to be dependent on the functional structures of CDs, because the electronic localized states of CDs imparted by their chemical moieties could dramatically change the distribution and migration of photocarriers within heterojunctions. Specifically, the surface emissive traps of CDs induced by carboxyl groups or pyrrolic-N groups was beneficial to attract photogenerated electrons from CN, which contributed to the photocarriers separation and electron transfer in the photocatalyst-driven PMS activation systems, while the electron-donating graphitic-N within CDs structures prefer to render the CDs to form electron-rich regions at the interface of CN host, thus making barriers against charge carriers separation in the photocatalytic PMS activation process. Based on the in-depth understanding of the dependence relationship between functional structure of CDs and photocatalytic activity of CDs/CN heterojunctions, this work will provide new insights into the structural design of high-performance heterojunctions based on CN and CDs.

Pei Yang: Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. An Shen: Investigation, Writing – original draft. Ziqi Zhu: Investigation. Ling Wang: Investigation. Ruixin Tang: Investigation. Kai Yang: Investigation. Minzhi Chen: Conceptualization, Methodology. Hongqi Dai: Conceptualization, Methodology. Xiaoyan Zhou: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Project administration.
